method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name *Hawaii* is thought to derive from Hawaiian *Hawai‘i*, ultimately from Proto-Polynesian *hawaiki*. [1][2] It is commonly explained as a reference to Hawaiki, the ancestral homeland of Polynesians, and some sources gloss it as meaning “Place of the Gods.” [1][2]

==Geography and environment==
Hawaii is an archipelago in the central Pacific Ocean made up of eight major islands and 124 minor islands, with a total land area of 6,425 square miles and a general coastline of about 750 miles.[2][4] The major islands, from west to east, are Niihau, Kauai, Oahu, Molokai, Lanai, Kahoolawe, Maui, and Hawaii.[5] The state stretches roughly 1,500 miles from Kure Island in the west to the island of Hawaii in the east, and its islands are the tops of volcanic mountains formed as the Pacific Plate moved over a hotspot beneath the ocean floor.[5] Honolulu is the state capital and is about 2,397 miles from San Francisco and 4,829 miles from Washington, D.C.[2][4]

The highest point in the state is Mauna Kea at 13,796 feet (4,205 m) above sea level.[2][4] Other notable geographic features include Kaukonahua Stream on Oahu, the state’s longest stream at 33 miles, Halalii on Niihau, the largest lake at 841 acres, and Kahiwa on Molokai, the highest named waterfall at 1,750 feet.[2][4] Hawaii’s topography also includes dramatic sea cliffs, especially along the northeastern coast of Molokai, which rise to about 3,250 feet and have been described as among the highest sea cliffs in the world.[4]

Because of its tropical location and varied elevation, Hawaii has highly diverse climatic and environmental conditions.[1][4] Recorded rainfall ranges from very low totals at some dry coastal sites to exceptionally high totals on windward mountain slopes such as Waialeale.[4] The state has also experienced major natural events, including long volcanic eruptions, strong earthquakes, and large tsunami waves.[4]

Environmental and resource management in Hawaii has long involved multiple state and federal agencies, including the U.S. Geological Survey, the National Ocean Survey, the National Weather Service, and the Hawaii state departments responsible for land, water, and health.[1][4] Measurements of water use and neighborhood noise levels have also been documented as part of the state’s broader geographic and environmental monitoring.[4]

==History==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians, who are thought to have arrived around 1,500 years ago using stars to navigate across the Pacific.[3][5] By the time of sustained European contact, the islands had developed into a complex chiefdom structure, and in 1778 Captain James Cook became the first documented European to reach the Hawaiian Islands at Waimea Bay on Kauaʻi.[3] Cook named the archipelago the “Sandwich Islands,” and his death at Kealakekua Bay a year later marked an early and dramatic chapter in Hawaiʻi’s modern history.[3]

In the late 18th century, warfare among the islands culminated in the rise of Kamehameha I, whose victories at battles such as Kepaniwai and Nuʻuanu helped him unite the Hawaiian Islands by 1810.[3] The Kamehameha dynasty then ruled the kingdom, and in 1819 Liholiho helped end the kapu system by publicly violating its rules on male and female dining.[3] The first Christian missionaries arrived in 1820, and Lahaina served as the capital of the Hawaiian Kingdom from 1820 to 1845.[3]

Sugar cultivation became increasingly important after the first sugar plantation opened on Kauaʻi in 1835, helping transform Hawaiʻi’s economy.[3] During the 19th and early 20th centuries, Hawaiʻi also experienced major immigration and labor changes, including Japanese emigration companies, attempts to secure Chinese contract labor, and the growth of a multicultural society shaped by migration and language contact.[1]

Modern scientific study has shown that the Hawaiian Islands were formed by volcanic activity over a hot spot in the Pacific Plate, with the Big Island of Hawaiʻi the youngest part of the chain and older islands and seamounts stretching northwestward.[2][4] The volcanic chain extends from the active islands and seamounts of the Hawaiian-Emperor system to much older submerged features, reflecting the islands’ long geological history.[2][4] In geological terms, the archipelago’s origins reach back tens of millions of years, while present-day volcanism continues on Hawaiʻi Island at Mauna Loa, Kīlauea, and the Lōʻihi Seamount.[4]

Hawaiʻi became the 50th state of the United States on August 21, 1959.[4]

==Demographics==
Hawaii’s population was estimated at 1,441,553 in 2021, making it the 30th most populous U.S. state.[1] The state experienced a population increase of 25,681 people, or 1.8%, from 2019 to 2021.[1] Most residents live on Oʻahu, which accounted for 69.4% of the state population, while Hawaii Island, Maui County, and Kauaʻi County accounted for 14.1%, 11.4%, and 5.1%, respectively.[1]

Recent U.S. Census estimates place Hawaii’s population at 1,446,146 in July 2024, down slightly from the 2020 census count of 1,455,271.[5] In 2024, 20.3% of residents were under age 18 and 21.5% were age 65 or older, while females made up 49.9% of the population.[5] The state’s median age was 41.0 years in 2024.[2]

Hawaii is one of the most ethnically diverse states in the United States.[1][5] In 2024, the racial composition included 25.1% White alone, 37.4% Asian alone, 10.4% Native Hawaiian and other Pacific Islander alone, and 24.5% two or more races; Hispanic or Latino residents comprised 10.2% of the population.[5] Among Asian groups, Japanese and Chinese were among the largest communities, with Japanese alone or in combination accounting for 21.8% and Chinese alone or in combination for 15.3% of the population in 2021.[1]

Native Hawaiians are a significant part of the state’s demographic profile.[4] One analysis states that Native Hawaiians made up about 6% of the islands’ population, or 21% when including those of part-Hawaiian ancestry.[4] The Native Hawaiian population has also been projected to continue growing in the coming decades.[4]

Immigration has also shaped Hawaii’s demographics.[1][5] Based on the 2021 American Community Survey, Hawaii had 270,345 immigrants, or 18.8% of its residents, the sixth-highest share among the states.[1] In 2020–2024, the U.S. Census Bureau estimated that 17.9% of Hawaii’s residents were foreign-born.[5]

==Economy==
Hawaiʻi’s economy is tracked and analyzed by the state’s Research & Economic Analysis Division, which collects economic data, publishes forecasts and reports, and provides tools and dashboards on subjects including tourism, labor, energy, population, and industry trends.[1] The division also serves as the state’s officially designated Census Data center and regularly issues publications on economic conditions and emerging industries.[1]  

Tourism remains a central part of the economy, and recent state reporting shows mixed conditions: visitor expenditures and transient accommodation tax revenues rose, while visitor arrivals declined in the most recent quarter reported.[3] In the fourth quarter of 2025, visitor arrivals by air fell by 3.2 percent compared with a year earlier, even as visitor expenditures increased by 8.5 percent.[3]  

Labor-market conditions have been relatively steady, with the civilian labor force and non-agricultural wage and salary employment both increasing in late 2025.[3] State labor data for early 2026 showed a civilian labor force of about 687,800, employment of about 672,200, unemployment of about 15,700, and an unemployment rate of 2.3 percent.[4] Total nonfarm employment was about 643,700 in February 2026, up 0.3 percent from a year earlier.[4]  

Hawaiʻi’s economy is also characterized by a high degree of industry concentration compared with the United States as a whole.[2] In 2020, the state’s top five industry groups accounted for 62.7 percent of total nominal GDP, compared with 53.4 percent for the same five sectors nationally.[2] The largest gap between Hawaiʻi and the U.S. was in government, where the state’s GDP share exceeded the national share by 9.7 percentage points.[2]  

From 2010 to 2020, Hawaiʻi’s real GDP increased only slightly overall, rising by about $1.1 billion, or 1.6 percent.[2] Much of that decade’s weakness reflected a $8.5 billion, or 10.7 percent, decline from 2019 to 2020 associated with the COVID-19 pandemic.[2]

==Culture==
Hawaiʻi’s culture is the product of many layers of cultural exchange, shaped by Native Hawaiian traditions, colonial disruption, and later revival movements.[1] For much of the 19th and 20th centuries, colonial influences diminished Native Hawaiian language and customs, but a cultural renaissance beginning in the 1970s helped restore and strengthen traditional practices.[1] This revival emphasized Hawaiian-language instruction and renewed interest in voyaging canoes, tattooing, kapa making, traditional music, and especially hula.[1]  

Traditional Hawaiian practices remain visible in everyday life, including the widespread use of Hawaiian words and the giving of the lei, while the “Aloha Spirit” is often described as reflecting the way diverse communities live together across the islands.[1] Hawaiʻi is also home to major cultural institutions such as the Bernice P. Bishop Museum and the Honolulu Academy of Arts, which preserve and display the history, arts, and sciences of Hawaiʻi and the broader Pacific.[1]  

The islands’ cultural history also includes major social transformations under the Hawaiian monarchy, particularly during the reign of Queen Kaʻahumanu, whose actions significantly reshaped Hawaiian society and religious life.[2] More broadly, scholars of Hawaiian history and prehistory describe the development of Hawaiian culture from Polynesian settlement through the formation of complex social, political, religious, and material traditions.[3][4]  

Modern Hawaiʻi is marked by cultural diversity, and the traditions of many ethnic groups have become part of mainstream life, including Chinese New Year celebrations and the Japanese Bon festival.[1] At the same time, Native Hawaiian communities continue to emphasize culturally grounded approaches to identity and psychology, situating Hawaiian experience within ecological, historical, and cultural contexts.[4] Tourism and public presentation of Hawaiʻi’s culture also draw heavily on Hawaiian traditions, especially performances and immersive experiences that aim to communicate historical narratives, spirituality, and community values.[5]

==Tourism==
Tourism is the activity most responsible for Hawaii’s current economic growth and standard of living, and it remains a central part of the state’s economy.[2] The visitor industry also has major effects on Hawaii’s physical infrastructure, natural resources, and social and cultural life.[2] State tourism planning and research have focused on understanding how tourism growth can be sustained while protecting economic, environmental, and socio-cultural assets.[2][4] Hawaii’s tourism data publications track indicators such as visitor arrivals, visitor spending, state tax revenue, average daily census, and air service capacity.[1][5]

Tourism in Hawaii has been shaped by both economic expansion and concerns about sustainability.[2][3] As visitor numbers increase, tourism can contribute to urbanization and land development, including hotel construction and related infrastructure.[3] These changes have raised concerns about human-environment interaction, environmental degradation, and the loss of scenic landscapes.[3] Tourism has also been linked to pressure on local culture and community life, including concerns about disrespect for Native Hawaiian traditions and cultural appropriation.[3]

Research by the state has also examined the socio-cultural impacts of tourism, including its possible relationship to crime and other community impacts.[4] That work found that the evidence was complex and did not produce a single clear conclusion, but it emphasized that tourism can create mixed benefits and costs for residents.[4] More broadly, the literature on Hawaii tourism has highlighted tensions between economic gains and the distribution of social and cultural impacts.[4]

Recent state reporting shows that Hawaii’s tourism industry remains large even after major fluctuations in the pandemic period.[5] In calendar year 2024, total visitor spending was $20.68 billion, and visitor arrivals reached 9,657,607 in 2023, both figures illustrating the continuing scale of the visitor economy.[5] Air service capacity remains an important measure of tourism access and demand, with quarterly reporting showing changes in total, domestic, and international seats available to Hawaii.[1]

==Health==
Hawaii’s health profile is documented through a range of state and federal data sources, including the Hawaii Department of Health, the CDC, the National Center for Health Statistics, and the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse.[1][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey used to provide statistics for planning, evaluation, and research on health services and programs.[4] It collects core demographic and health variables such as age, gender, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and both mental and physical health status.[4]  

State health datasets also track topics such as immunizations, infectious diseases, chronic disease indicators, and county-level health measures.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse and related state health portals compile information from surveys including the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2][4] These sources are used to monitor patterns in adult and adolescent health, including obesity and major depressive episodes among teenagers.[3]  

Public health reporting in Hawaii also highlights population-specific issues, especially for women and older adults.[1] The state maintains data resources on women’s health, aging, and aging-related services through agencies such as the Hawaii State Commission on the Status of Women, the Executive Office on Aging, and the University of Hawaii at Mānoa Center on the Family.[1]  

Historical scholarship on Hawaiian health emphasizes the long-term effects of Western contact on Native Hawaiian population size and health conditions.[5] It also notes continuing efforts to improve care for Native Hawaiian and traditional patients and to provide health providers with historical and cultural context.[5]

==Education==
Hawaii’s public education system is administered as a single statewide district, so it is not included in district-by-district school finance grading. Its school finance profile has been described as comparatively strong on spending, earning a B-minus in that category, well above the national average of D.[1]

In broader assessments of state education outcomes, Hawaii has ranked 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall score of 75.3 out of 100 and a grade of C.[1] In the same assessment, Hawaii received a D-minus for current student performance, ranking 41st nationally, while its measured improvement over time earned a C-minus and a 10th-place ranking.[1]

Education in Hawaii also includes efforts to incorporate Native Hawaiian language and culture into teaching. Research on culturally based education in the state has examined Hawaiian language use, Hawaiian-language instructional materials, and community involvement as part of a broader framework for engaging students.[4]

The University of Hawaiʻi system is a major part of higher education in the state. It consists of three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers on six islands, and Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in fall 2017 and 21.3% of degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]

Stanford University’s Wrigley Field Program in Hawaii has also used the islands as an outdoor classroom for field-based science education, with students studying vegetation, coral reefs, and volcanoes to better understand ecological and human-environment interactions.[3]

==Transportation==
Transportation in Hawaiʻi accounts for about 55% of the state’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest shares.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence is a central goal of Hawaiʻi’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience efforts.[5] State analyses of clean-energy transportation policy emphasize two broad approaches: shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing overall transportation energy demand.[1][5]

Because transportation electrification is expected to raise electricity demand, planners in Hawaiʻi have stressed the need for coordinated planning across the transportation and energy systems.[2][5] Research on electric vehicles in the state has found that adoption is strongly associated with income, educational attainment, and access to charging infrastructure.[3] Hawaiʻi has also been identified as an important case for EV policy because of its island geography, high fuel costs, and the potential for renewable electricity to lower transportation emissions.[2][3]

Efforts to cut transportation energy use have also focused on reducing vehicle miles traveled, which have increased significantly over time as population growth and higher vehicle ownership outweighed stable per-vehicle driving levels.[1] Broader state transportation studies have likewise examined how infrastructure access, equity, and local market conditions affect participation in transportation-related industries and services.[4] Aviation and maritime transport remain especially important in Hawaiʻi because of the state’s dependence on imported goods and interisland and long-distance travel.[5]

==Governance==
Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians between about A.D. 300 and 600, and by around A.D. 600 to 1000 a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, belief system, and social order had developed.[1] Native Hawaiian governance is described in oral traditions and genealogies as a long-standing system of chiefly rule that predates contact with the United States by many centuries.[1][3] Some Hawaiian historians and genealogists place the beginning of ruling chiefs at roughly 2,000 years before present, while other analyses trace chiefly lines forward from later migration periods into the era of Kamehameha I.[1][3]

By the late 18th century, the islands were divided among various chiefs until Kamehameha I unified them and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[2] The kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 Kamehameha III promulgated the first written constitution, transforming it into a constitutional monarchy.[2] Subsequent constitutions limited monarchical power further, and Queen Liliʻuokalani was the last reigning monarch before the overthrow of the kingdom.[2]

Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894, was annexed by the United States in 1898, became a U.S. territory in 1900, and entered the Union as the 50th state in 1959.[2] Scholarly summaries of Native Hawaiian governance emphasize that Native Hawaiians were the indigenous people of the islands and had exercised sovereignty and self-governance continuously through the Hawaiian Kingdom, the constitutional monarchy, and beyond.[3][5] These accounts also stress that Native Hawaiian self-governance became distinct from the governance of Hawaiʻi’s broader multi-ethnic population in the late 19th century and continues as a separate political and cultural tradition.[3][5]

Traditional Hawaiian political structure was stratified by genealogy and rank, with higher-ranking chiefs such as aliʻi nui or mōʻi governing whole islands or districts and lesser chiefs and konohiki managing smaller land units.[4] An ʻaha aliʻi of high chiefs advised in political matters, and governance also involved the office of kuhina nui, which in the early monarchy was held by women before later constitutional changes excluded them from political proceedings.[4] During the transition to constitutional monarchy, voting and political participation increasingly became restricted by gender, literacy, tax, and property requirements, and missionary influence and Euro-American legal models further altered Hawaiian governance.[4]

==Hawaiian sovereignty movement==
The Hawaiian sovereignty movement emerged in the early 1970s and developed into a broad set of political and cultural efforts by Native Hawaiians seeking self-determination, land stewardship, and redress for the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom.[1][4][5]  

Its goals range from cultural revitalization and local control over land and resources to legal recognition of Hawaiian nationhood, independence, or other forms of political sovereignty.[1][2][3]  

Scholars and activists note that the movement grew alongside other political currents of the period, including ethnic studies, civil rights, women’s liberation, student activism, and antiwar protest in Hawaiʻi.[1][4]  

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the movement had become a more visible and widely discussed political force, with some observers describing it as a major catalyst for change in Hawaiian politics.[4]  

The movement has included a variety of organizations and approaches, from groups pursuing international legal remedies and decolonization to others emphasizing reparations or restoration of an independent Hawaiian government.[2][3][5]  

Important episodes often associated with the movement include the Kalama Valley, Niumalu, Heʻeia Kea, Waiāhole/Waiākane, Hilo Airport, and Hale Mohalu struggles, as well as opposition to U.S. military use of Kahoʻolawe.[1]  

Because it encompasses both political and cultural claims, the movement has remained diverse, contested, and central to debates over sovereignty, self-governance, and Hawaiian identity in modern Hawaiʻi.[1][2][4][5]

==International sister relationships==
Hawaiʻi’s Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism maintains the state’s sister-state program, which was created to foster long-term relationships that provide cultural, political, and economic benefits, and is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2 and the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission.[1] The program’s stated purpose is to support the governor, legislature, and Office of International Affairs in building strategic partnerships that enrich the people, cultures, environments, and economies of Hawaiʻi and its international partners.[1] Hawaiʻi’s sister-state policy emphasizes that successful relationships should have commonality with Hawaiʻi, particularly through geographic, historical, cultural, or economic ties, and applicants must provide a governor’s letter, a designated point of contact, recent activity reports, and recommendations for future activities.[2] The state periodically reviews these relationships to determine which ties should be strengthened or ended.[2]

As of the 2025 report, Hawaiʻi had 19 sister-state relationships.[2] These include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; the Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993).[1] A later report also noted that some relationships, including Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong, have been especially active.[2]

Hawaiʻi’s international sister relationships are also reflected in educational and cultural exchanges. For example, the Hawaiʻi Global Education Foundation reported on efforts connected to the Hawaiʻi–Fukuoka relationship, including commemorative plaques presented to honor prominent individuals with ties to Fukuoka and exchanges intended to strengthen ties between Hawaiʻi and Japan.[3] At the county level, Hawaiʻi County has also developed sister-city partnerships in the Indo-Pacific, including a relationship with Hatsukaichi, Japan, that was described as rooted in shared natural beauty, cultural heritage, and familial links to Hiroshima Prefecture.[4]

Tourism in Hawaii has been shaped by both economic expansion and concerns about sustainability.[2][3] As visitor numbers increase, tourism can contribute to urbanization and land development, including hotel construction and related infrastructure.[3] These changes have raised concerns about human-environment interaction, environmental degradation, and the loss of scenic landscapes.[3] Tourism has also been linked to pressure on local culture and community life, including concerns about disrespect for Native Hawaiian traditions and cultural appropriation.[3]

Research by the state has also examined the socio-cultural impacts of tourism, including its possible relationship to crime and other community impacts.[4] That work found that the evidence was complex and did not produce a single clear conclusion, but it emphasized that tourism can create mixed benefits and costs for residents.[4] More broadly, the literature on Hawaii tourism has highlighted tensions between economic gains and the distribution of social and cultural impacts.[4]

Recent state reporting shows that Hawaii’s tourism industry remains large even after major fluctuations in the pandemic period.[5] In calendar year 2024, total visitor spending was $20.68 billion, and visitor arrivals reached 9,657,607 in 2023, both figures illustrating the continuing scale of the visitor economy.[5] Air service capacity remains an important measure of tourism access and demand, with quarterly reporting showing changes in total, domestic, and international seats available to Hawaii.[1]

Hawaii’s health profile is documented through a range of state and federal data sources, including the Hawaii Department of Health, the CDC, the National Center for Health Statistics, and the Hawaii Health Data Warehouse.[1][4] The Hawaii Health Survey is a continuous statewide household survey used to provide statistics for planning, evaluation, and research on health services and programs.[4] It collects core demographic and health variables such as age, gender, income, ethnicity, education, household size, insurance status, poverty status, and both mental and physical health status.[4]  

State health datasets also track topics such as immunizations, infectious diseases, chronic disease indicators, and county-level health measures.[2] The Hawaii Health Data Warehouse and related state health portals compile information from surveys including the Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System, the Pregnancy Risk Assessment Monitoring System, and the Youth Risk Behavior Survey.[2][4] These sources are used to monitor patterns in adult and adolescent health, including obesity and major depressive episodes among teenagers.[3]  

Public health reporting in Hawaii also highlights population-specific issues, especially for women and older adults.[1] The state maintains data resources on women’s health, aging, and aging-related services through agencies such as the Hawaii State Commission on the Status of Women, the Executive Office on Aging, and the University of Hawaii at Mānoa Center on the Family.[1]  

Historical scholarship on Hawaiian health emphasizes the long-term effects of Western contact on Native Hawaiian population size and health conditions.[5] It also notes continuing efforts to improve care for Native Hawaiian and traditional patients and to provide health providers with historical and cultural context.[5]

Hawaii’s public education system is administered as a single statewide district, so it is not included in district-by-district school finance grading. Its school finance profile has been described as comparatively strong on spending, earning a B-minus in that category, well above the national average of D.[1]

In broader assessments of state education outcomes, Hawaii has ranked 22nd overall among the 50 states and the District of Columbia, with an overall score of 75.3 out of 100 and a grade of C.[1] In the same assessment, Hawaii received a D-minus for current student performance, ranking 41st nationally, while its measured improvement over time earned a C-minus and a 10th-place ranking.[1]

Education in Hawaii also includes efforts to incorporate Native Hawaiian language and culture into teaching. Research on culturally based education in the state has examined Hawaiian language use, Hawaiian-language instructional materials, and community involvement as part of a broader framework for engaging students.[4]

The University of Hawaiʻi system is a major part of higher education in the state. It consists of three universities, seven community colleges, and nine education centers on six islands, and Native Hawaiian students made up 23.3% of total enrollment in fall 2017 and 21.3% of degrees and certificates awarded in fiscal year 2017–2018.[5]

Stanford University’s Wrigley Field Program in Hawaii has also used the islands as an outdoor classroom for field-based science education, with students studying vegetation, coral reefs, and volcanoes to better understand ecological and human-environment interactions.[3]

Transportation in Hawaiʻi accounts for about 55% of the state’s total energy use, with aviation and ground transportation making up the largest shares.[5] Reducing petroleum dependence is a central goal of Hawaiʻi’s long-term energy planning and economic resilience efforts.[5] State analyses of clean-energy transportation policy emphasize two broad approaches: shifting away from petroleum-fueled vehicles and improving system efficiency by reducing overall transportation energy demand.[1][5]

Because transportation electrification is expected to raise electricity demand, planners in Hawaiʻi have stressed the need for coordinated planning across the transportation and energy systems.[2][5] Research on electric vehicles in the state has found that adoption is strongly associated with income, educational attainment, and access to charging infrastructure.[3] Hawaiʻi has also been identified as an important case for EV policy because of its island geography, high fuel costs, and the potential for renewable electricity to lower transportation emissions.[2][3]

Efforts to cut transportation energy use have also focused on reducing vehicle miles traveled, which have increased significantly over time as population growth and higher vehicle ownership outweighed stable per-vehicle driving levels.[1] Broader state transportation studies have likewise examined how infrastructure access, equity, and local market conditions affect participation in transportation-related industries and services.[4] Aviation and maritime transport remain especially important in Hawaiʻi because of the state’s dependence on imported goods and interisland and long-distance travel.[5]

Hawaiʻi was first settled by Polynesians between about A.D. 300 and 600, and by around A.D. 600 to 1000 a distinct Native Hawaiian language, culture, belief system, and social order had developed.[1] Native Hawaiian governance is described in oral traditions and genealogies as a long-standing system of chiefly rule that predates contact with the United States by many centuries.[1][3] Some Hawaiian historians and genealogists place the beginning of ruling chiefs at roughly 2,000 years before present, while other analyses trace chiefly lines forward from later migration periods into the era of Kamehameha I.[1][3]

By the late 18th century, the islands were divided among various chiefs until Kamehameha I unified them and established the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi.[2] The kingdom initially functioned as an absolute monarchy, but in 1840 Kamehameha III promulgated the first written constitution, transforming it into a constitutional monarchy.[2] Subsequent constitutions limited monarchical power further, and Queen Liliʻuokalani was the last reigning monarch before the overthrow of the kingdom.[2]

Hawaiʻi became a republic on July 4, 1894, was annexed by the United States in 1898, became a U.S. territory in 1900, and entered the Union as the 50th state in 1959.[2] Scholarly summaries of Native Hawaiian governance emphasize that Native Hawaiians were the indigenous people of the islands and had exercised sovereignty and self-governance continuously through the Hawaiian Kingdom, the constitutional monarchy, and beyond.[3][5] These accounts also stress that Native Hawaiian self-governance became distinct from the governance of Hawaiʻi’s broader multi-ethnic population in the late 19th century and continues as a separate political and cultural tradition.[3][5]

Traditional Hawaiian political structure was stratified by genealogy and rank, with higher-ranking chiefs such as aliʻi nui or mōʻi governing whole islands or districts and lesser chiefs and konohiki managing smaller land units.[4] An ʻaha aliʻi of high chiefs advised in political matters, and governance also involved the office of kuhina nui, which in the early monarchy was held by women before later constitutional changes excluded them from political proceedings.[4] During the transition to constitutional monarchy, voting and political participation increasingly became restricted by gender, literacy, tax, and property requirements, and missionary influence and Euro-American legal models further altered Hawaiian governance.[4]

The Hawaiian sovereignty movement emerged in the early 1970s and developed into a broad set of political and cultural efforts by Native Hawaiians seeking self-determination, land stewardship, and redress for the 1893 overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom.[1][4][5]  

Its goals range from cultural revitalization and local control over land and resources to legal recognition of Hawaiian nationhood, independence, or other forms of political sovereignty.[1][2][3]  

Scholars and activists note that the movement grew alongside other political currents of the period, including ethnic studies, civil rights, women’s liberation, student activism, and antiwar protest in Hawaiʻi.[1][4]  

By the late 1980s and early 1990s, the movement had become a more visible and widely discussed political force, with some observers describing it as a major catalyst for change in Hawaiian politics.[4]  

The movement has included a variety of organizations and approaches, from groups pursuing international legal remedies and decolonization to others emphasizing reparations or restoration of an independent Hawaiian government.[2][3][5]  

Important episodes often associated with the movement include the Kalama Valley, Niumalu, Heʻeia Kea, Waiāhole/Waiākane, Hilo Airport, and Hale Mohalu struggles, as well as opposition to U.S. military use of Kahoʻolawe.[1]  

Because it encompasses both political and cultural claims, the movement has remained diverse, contested, and central to debates over sovereignty, self-governance, and Hawaiian identity in modern Hawaiʻi.[1][2][4][5]

Hawaiʻi’s Department of Business, Economic Development and Tourism maintains the state’s sister-state program, which was created to foster long-term relationships that provide cultural, political, and economic benefits, and is governed by Hawaiʻi Revised Statutes Chapter 229-2 and the Hawaiʻi Sister-State and International Partnerships Commission.[1] The program’s stated purpose is to support the governor, legislature, and Office of International Affairs in building strategic partnerships that enrich the people, cultures, environments, and economies of Hawaiʻi and its international partners.[1] Hawaiʻi’s sister-state policy emphasizes that successful relationships should have commonality with Hawaiʻi, particularly through geographic, historical, cultural, or economic ties, and applicants must provide a governor’s letter, a designated point of contact, recent activity reports, and recommendations for future activities.[2] The state periodically reviews these relationships to determine which ties should be strengthened or ended.[2]

As of the 2025 report, Hawaiʻi had 19 sister-state relationships.[2] These include Guangdong (1985) and Hainan (1992) in China; Bali (2014) in Indonesia; Fukuoka (1981), Okinawa (1985), Hiroshima (1997), Ehime (2003), Hokkaido (2017), and Yamaguchi (2022) in Japan; Jeju (1986) in Korea; Rabat-Sale-Zemmour-Zaer (2011) in Morocco; Ilocos Sur (1985), Cebu (1996), Pangasinan (2002), Ilocos Norte (2005), and Isabela (2006) in the Philippines; the Azores Islands (1982) in Portugal; and Taiwan (1993).[1] A later report also noted that some relationships, including Hiroshima, Fukuoka, Okinawa, Taiwan, and Guangdong, have been especially active.[2]

Hawaiʻi’s international sister relationships are also reflected in educational and cultural exchanges. For example, the Hawaiʻi Global Education Foundation reported on efforts connected to the Hawaiʻi–Fukuoka relationship, including commemorative plaques presented to honor prominent individuals with ties to Fukuoka and exchanges intended to strengthen ties between Hawaiʻi and Japan.[3] At the county level, Hawaiʻi County has also developed sister-city partnerships in the Indo-Pacific, including a relationship with Hatsukaichi, Japan, that was described as rooted in shared natural beauty, cultural heritage, and familial links to Hiroshima Prefecture.[4]
